title: Chip-carved beech butter dish
subtitle: A flat serving dish with a simple geometric chip-carved border
excerpt: A rectangular butter dish in seasoned beech, decorated with a repeating chip-carved triangle border around the rim. The dish itself is shaped by push cuts and sanding; the chip-carving decorates the flat upper face before the oil is applied. A good second whittling project after a flat spatula or letter opener.
sourceNotes: Chip-carving border patterns documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in Scandinavian folk-craft woodwork traditions.

content.text: Chip carving is an old style of decorative woodwork. It needs a sharp knife, a flat
seasoned

surface, and a steady hand to keep each cut vertical. This butter dish is a flat rectangle, shaped by knife and sandpaper. The chip-carved triangle border is worked on the upper face after shaping, before the dish is oiled.
What you need: wood

A seasoned beech board section, 22 cm long, 10 cm wide, 2 cm thick. Beech produces a fine, even grain at the surface when sanded, which makes chip-carved triangles crisp and legible. The flat surface needs to be truly flat before chip carving begins; a warped or twisted board will cause the stop cuts to be inconsistent in depth. Run a straight edge across the board before starting; if there is noticeable rocking, select a different blank.

Lime is a good alternative for chip carving. It has the finest and most even grain of the food-safe hardwoods, and the cuts come out crisp. The finished surface is a little softer than beech but very consistent. Sycamore also works well. Avoid coarse-grained woods like oak and ash; the open grain spoils the chip walls.
What you need: tools

A chip-carving knife with a 65-degree point, held with the thumb on the spine for control; this is the primary chip-carving tool. A sloyd knife for shaping the dish profile. A detail knife for very tight triangles where the chip-carving knife tip cannot reach the corner cleanly. A
strop

for both knives. Sandpaper in 120, 180, and 240 grit. Board butter for the finish; it is immediate-use after application (no cure time), food-safe, and appropriate for a piece that will be handled but not immersed in water.
Workspace

A stable flat surface to lay the dish on during chip carving. The dish must not rock; if it does, place a thin folded cloth under the low end to level it. Good direct overhead light is more important for chip carving than for most wood-craft work; the angled facets of the triangles only show clearly when lit from above. A low-angle bench light alongside is useful for checking triangle depth during work.
The work
Step 1: Shape the dish profile

Mark and cut the dish outline: a rectangle 20 cm by 8 cm with rounded corners. The rounding of the corners takes about 2 cm of each corner and creates a gentle oval at each end. Work the corners with the sloyd knife using push cuts along the edge, taking thin slices to bring the corner to the pencilled curve. Sand the face and edges to 120 grit to remove all knife marks and give a flat surface for the pencilled chip-carving pattern.
Step 2: Mark the chip-carved border

Pencil a border on the upper face of the dish, 1.5 cm in from the edge on all sides. This border zone will hold the chip-carved triangle pattern. Within the border zone, draw a series of equilateral triangles, each with a base on the inner border line and a point on the outer. A 12 mm equilateral triangle fits neatly in a 1.5 cm band. Mark all triangles with a ruler and a 60-degree set square before cutting any of them.
Step 3: Work the chip-carved triangles
For each triangle, make three
stop cuts

first: one along each side of the triangle, blade vertical and pressed 2 to 3 mm deep, angled very slightly inward toward the centre of the triangle. Then make three shaping cuts toward the centre of the triangle, each angled to meet the others at the same point and depth in the middle. If all three cuts are at the correct angle and depth, the
chip
lifts as one clean piece. If the cuts do not meet, deepen whichever is the shallowest and try again.

Work all the triangles in the border in the same session if possible; stopping midway and returning later often means hand and eye are slightly different in feel, which shows in the triangle depth consistency. Leave the flat centre of the dish uncarved; the chip pattern frames the plain centre.
Step 4: Sand to finish

Sand the flat centre and the edges from 180 to 240 grit. Do not sand the chip-carved border with abrasive paper; sanding removes the crisp chip facets that make the pattern legible. If pencil lines remain in the chip-carved sections, remove them with a soft eraser. Any pencil lines on the flat centre will sand away with the 240-grit pass.
Finishing

Apply board butter with a soft cloth, working it into the chip-carved recesses as well as the flat surfaces. Allow to stand for twenty minutes, then buff off firmly with a dry cloth. Board butter is immediately food-safe and requires no cure period. Re-apply monthly for a dish in regular use.
Variations
Use triangles pointing in and out along the border to make a zig-zag band, instead of a one-direction repeat.
Add a central motif in the plain dish centre: a simple six-pointed star or a single large triangle, chip-carved. Mark it carefully with dividers before cutting.

Make the dish circular (17 cm diameter) rather than rectangular. The chip-carved border follows the curved rim; this requires triangles of varying width to fill the curved border zone, which is more challenging to lay out.
Care

Wipe clean with a damp cloth; do not immerse in water. Dry immediately after wiping. Re-apply board butter monthly. The chip-carved recesses will accumulate oils from butter and cheese over time and will darken slightly, which deepens the visual contrast with the surrounding wood surface.

attrs.intro: Problems in the chip carving and shaping.

items.fix: Identify which cut is the shallowest by looking at the break line. Deepen that cut and try to lift the remainder cleanly. For future triangles, mark a centre depth target with a pencil dot in the middle of each triangle before cutting.
items.cause: The three shaping cuts did not meet at the same depth in the centre of the triangle.
items.symptom: The chip does not lift clean; it breaks partway and leaves a ridge in the centre

items.fix: Strop the knife. Try the shaping cut from the opposite corner; the grain direction varies in a chip triangle and one direction will be cleaner.
items.cause: The chip-carving knife is cutting against the grain, or the edge is dull.
items.symptom: The chip facets are rough rather than smooth

items.fix: Before pressing the stop cut, check that the blade is truly vertical by eye from two directions. The most common error is tilting forward in the direction of the next cut; resist this tendency.
items.cause: The blade was not held perpendicular to the wood surface during the stop cuts.
items.symptom: The stop cuts are sloped rather than vertical, leaving angled rather than flat chip walls

attrs.heading: Common problems
